demos.evidence_text: This month so far she has no seizures; earlier 4 in February, 0 in January and 7 in December
demos.note_text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 24 March 2024

Dr Wang
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL

Dear Dr Wang

John Doe, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: P546484 NHS No. 5484656746
Flat 15 Roundwood Road, London, E14 7JL

Thank you for referring and allowing me to review the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. She reports ongoing challenges associated with awaiting relocation from temporary accommodation; crowded living conditions noted, which she feels may be impacting sleep quality and stress levels.

Diagnosis and current status: She has focal epilepsy. Her semiology comprises a brief epigastric rising sensation followed by behavioural arrest and right-hand automatisms, occasionally progressing to impaired awareness; no recent generalised convulsions reported. This month so far she has no seizures; earlier 4 in February, 0 in January and 7 in December, over waking hours and sleep. She keeps a seizure diary that aligns with this account.

Antiseizure medications: Currently taking lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily. Previously trialled carbamazepine (discontinued due to rash) and topiramate (stopped for cognitive slowing). She reports mild irritability on levetiracetam but prefers to continue as seizure control has improved. No falls or injuries. No focal neurological complaints between events.

Investigations and plan: Prior MRI head (2022) reported left mesial temporal sclerosis; EEG (2023) showed left temporal interictal sharp waves. Given recent improvement and stable tolerability, I have not altered medication today. We discussed optimising sleep hygiene within current constraints and continuing her seizure diary. I have provided information on local epilepsy support services, which she may contact if helpful. We agreed to review in three months or sooner if seizures increase, new features emerge, or adverse effects occur.

Yours sincerely

St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 9 May 2017 

Dr Ahmed  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Ahmed,  

Mateo Alvarez, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: H938271 NHS No. 5839201746  
12 Brookfield Road, London, W10 5RA 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, commenced following initial assessment in primary care after recent events. Patient reports good adherence, with occasional missed morning doses during shift work. No notable adverse effects beyond mild irritability in the first fortnight, now settled. 

History and interval events: 

Mateo Alvarez is a recent migrant to the UK and is still learning how to navigate NHS services; he has occasionally attended with interpreter support. He has received our written safety advice translated into his preferred language and confirms understanding. Since initial review by the GP, the pattern of episodes has been clarified as follows: In November he presented with a cluster of three seizures (self-terminating, fluctuating awareness). In February a nocturnal seizure were noted, and in April a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurred (not witnessed by family). No clear precipitants identified; he reports variable sleep due to new employment and some stress around housing paperwork. There is no history of significant head injury, CNS infection, or family history of similar episodes. 

Examination today is unremarkable with normal cardiorespiratory and neurological findings. He is oriented, with no focal deficits. 

Investigations: 

- Baseline bloods in primary care reported as within reference ranges (U&E, LFT, FBC).  
- We have arranged a routine EEG and MRI brain with epilepsy protocol; appointments have been requested with attention to interpreter availability.  
- DVLA guidance and safety precautions (bathing/swimming supervision, heights, machinery) discussed and provided in writing; he confirms he will refrain from driving and has initiated self-notification.  

Plan: 

1) Continue levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily for now; we will review dosing after EEG/MRI and in light of any further events.  
2) Seizure diary provided; he will record sleep patterns, missed doses, and any alcohol intake to help identify triggers.  
3) Written first aid guidance shared with family; he has consented to us sending a summary to his workplace occupational health.  
4) Interpreter-supported follow-up booked for 12 weeks, sooner if further seizures occur or adverse effects develop.  
5) Signposted to local community support and an information session on accessing NHS appointments, including how to request interpreters in advance.  

We will update you when investigations are available. Please contact us if there are interim concerns or if further events occur. 

Yours sincerely

demos.evidence_text: one drop attacks and four absence seizures in the past two months
demos.note_text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Fiona
Centre for Epilepsy
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Fiona,

Susan Miller, DOB: 14-05-1986, Hospital No: P948572 NHS No. 7623549812
12 Baker Street, London, NW1 6XE

Epilepsy

Medication: Ongoing Lamotrigine since February 2025

Lamotrigine tablets 100 mg twice a day

Lamotrigine Blood Level:

24 September 2025: 4.3 mg/L (normal range 1.0 to 15.0 mg/L). Dose 100 mg twice a day

Seizures: The patient reports one drop attacks and four absence seizures in the past two months. These were partly corroborated by smartwatch fall-detection alerts and activity logs, which the patient shared during review. The drop event triggered an automatic alert at home without injury. Absence episodes were noted by family as brief unresponsiveness with immediate recovery, without clear triggers.

I reviewed Susan Miller today in a face-to-face appointment. They report good adherence to lamotrigine with no rash or notable adverse effects. Sleep has been variable due to shift work, and caffeine intake increased recently; both may be contributing factors per the patient’s account. Alcohol intake is minimal. No recent intercurrent illnesses. The smartwatch’s fall-detection timestamps align with the patient’s diary entries and may help clarify event chronology going forward. We discussed continuing to mark events using both the smartwatch app and a paper diary, including possible auras, duration, and recovery time.

Neurological examination today was non-focal. No tongue biting or tonic–clonic activity has been witnessed during the reported episodes. The patient does not drive and understands current DVLA guidance as discussed. We agreed to maintain the current lamotrigine dose pending further correlation of events and to obtain an updated EEG and routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC) alongside repeat lamotrigine levels if further events occur. Safety measures were reinforced, including use of the smartwatch fall alerts and avoiding high-risk situations when alone.

I will review again in three months or sooner if there are additional events. The patient has been asked to send interim updates via the patient portal, including any new smartwatch alert data.

Yours sincerely

demos.evidence_text: This month he experienced two clusters this month; each ~five focal impaired-awareness seizures
demos.note_text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St George's University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Neurology Outpatient Department, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

James Walker, DOB: 14-08-1987, Hospital No: H928374 NHS No. 7392015468  
12 Brookside Close, Wimbledon, London SW19 3DA  

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; aetiology and classification under ongoing evaluation. 

I reviewed James Walker in clinic today with his partner present. He remains engaged with a local peer support group, and observations from peers have supplemented his history between appointments. He reports increased reliability in tracking events using a seizure diary corroborated by his group facilitator. This month he experienced two clusters this month; each ~five focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically occurring over 24–36 hours, with brief post-ictal confusion and fatigue but no injuries. There have been no witnessed generalised tonic–clonic events, no myoclonic jerks, and no nocturnal tongue biting reported. 

Precipitants noted by the patient and peers include sleep deprivation and high work-related stress. Alcohol intake is minimal and unchanged. He denies missed medication doses. There is no new neurological symptomatology, and no intercurrent illness. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily continued without adverse effects beyond mild irritability, which he manages with scheduled exercise and mindfulness sessions recommended by the peer group. He carries buccal midazolam but has not required its use this year. 

Investigations: Previous MRI brain (2023) non-diagnostic. Interictal EEG (April 2024) showed non-specific changes without definitive lateralising or localising features. We have arranged an ambulatory EEG with event marker to capture a cluster if possible. Routine bloods today (U&E, LFT, FBC) were requested to monitor general health while on therapy. 

Plan: 
- Continue levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. 
- Proceed with ambulatory EEG; patient instructed to trigger the event marker at aura onset and obtain peer or partner corroboration where feasible. 
- Maintain seizure diary; continue engagement with the peer support group to enhance collateral reporting. 
- Safety advice re-emphasised: supervised bathing, cooking precautions, and avoidance of heights during periods of sleep disruption. Driving status remains suspended in accordance with DVLA guidance; we reviewed the requirements today. 
- Review in clinic in three months, or sooner if cluster frequency increases or if any generalised convulsive event occurs. 

Yours sincerely

fields.description: Seizure frequency in Gan label format. One of: '{count} per {unit}' e.g. '2 per week', '1 per 3 month'; '{count} cluster per {unit}, {n} per cluster'; 'unknown'; 'no seizure frequency reference'; or null to abstain. Unit must be singular: day/week/month/year.
Shortest exact contiguous quote from the note supporting the label. Do not include prompt instructions or wrapper text. Null if abstaining.

signature.instructions:
Extract the Gan seizure-frequency label and supporting evidence from a clinical note.

/no_think
Do not use hidden reasoning. Emit only the requested output fields.

The label must match the Gan annotation vocabulary exactly. Abstain (null) only when
the note contains no usable seizure-frequency information.

Synthesis-backed policy:
- Output only canonical Gan labels: N per unit, N to M per unit, N per M unit,
  N cluster per unit, M per cluster, seizure free for N unit, unknown, or
  no seizure frequency reference.
- Use singular units: day, week, month, year.
- Convert daily/nightly to 1 per day, every N unit to 1 per N unit, and
  numeric "or" ranges to "to" ranges.
- Choose the highest current quantified seizure-type frequency.
- Use the full cluster format; do not drop either the cluster period or
  the per-cluster count.
- Use seizure free for N unit only when seizure freedom is at least 6 months.
  If seizure freedom is shorter than 6 months, compute the rate from the
  total events over the described period.
- For year-to-date counts, use months elapsed since January as the denominator.
- Treat "over the last quarter" as an observation window when the note gives
  a per-month rate; do not automatically convert it to per 3 month.
- evidence_text must be an exact contiguous quote from the note.
